text: The surgeon performs central dissection for deep endometriosis, with the assistant providing cranial traction on the rectum. Dense endometriotic adhesions are visible without nodules on the rectovaginal septum. The posterior vagina and rectal fat are dissected down towards the anorectal junction.

The surgeon identifies the anorectal junction, revealing the serosal coat of the rectum densely adhered to the posterior vaginal wall. Using gentle traction and dissection with bipolar forceps and scissors, the surgeon carefully separates these structures. The rectovaginal septum is opened up as the dissection progresses away from the fibrotic planes.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the caudal direction, reaching the levator ani muscle, which lies laterally to the anorectal junction. Using bipolar coagulation and scissors, gentle traction is applied followed by sharp cuts to the fibrotic tissue. The dense fibrosis is visible along the pararectal fascia, requiring careful attention to potential bleeding.

The surgeon mobilizes the caudal part of the rectal pillar, coagulating it after initial mobilization. The rectum is completely mobilized away from the posterior fornix and posterior vaginal wall, revealing the longitudinal layer and the white anorectal junction. The anterior serosa of the rectum, forming the posterior part of the Danone-Willers fascia, is dissected and removed from the peritoneal cavity. The nerve-sparing technique preserves the rectal plexus from the inferior hypogastric nerve, visible on the right side. Gentle cuts are made over the serosal covering of the rectum to expose the longitudinal muscle.

The surgeon exposes the longitudinal muscle of the rectum by removing the overlying fat and the Danone-Willers fascia, leaving the lower one-third of the rectum bare. A rectal probe is inserted, and methylene blue is used to check the integrity of the rectum. The longitudinal muscle is clearly visible for approximately seven to eight centimeters, with nerve plexuses preserved.
